description:
This diagram illustrates the decomposition of an original interferogram into its constituent phase components and the subsequent extraction of the deformation signal, fundamental to Interferometric Synthetic Aperture Radar (InSAR) analysis.

The top row shows the "original interferogram" (a grayscale image) being mathematically decomposed into four additive components:
1.  "atmospheric phase screen" (a grayscale image with a large white region indicating atmospheric effects).
2.  "DEM related component" (a grayscale image showing relief, corresponding to the topographical phase `φ_t`).
3.  "deformation" (a grayscale image with subtle patterns, corresponding to the displacement phase `φ_d`).
4.  "noise" (an implicitly separate or combined component `ν`).

This decomposition aligns with the general InSAR phase equation: `original interferogram = atmospheric phase screen + DEM related component + deformation + noise`.

The bottom section illustrates the process of isolating the deformation signal. A large blue arrow points from the "original interferogram" (depicted with a satellite icon showing "azimuth" and "range" acquisition geometry) to a new image. This new image represents the "original interferogram" after subtracting the atmospheric and Digital Elevation Model (DEM) related phase components. The equation for this transformation is depicted as: `(original interferogram - atmospheric phase screen - DEM related component) = deformation + noise`. The resultant image on the right shows the combined "deformation + noise" component, where the effects of atmosphere and topography have been largely removed, highlighting the ground deformation.
